title: /24 CIDR to Subnet Mask Converter
seo_content:
A /24 subnet mask (255.255.255.0) is the standard for most local area networks (LANs). It provides 256 total IP addresses, with 254 available for devices after reserving the network and broadcast addresses.

This prefix is ideal for small businesses and home offices because it offers a perfect balance between network size and ease of management. In binary, the first 24 bits are set to 1, locking the network portion and leaving 8 bits for host identification.

title: /25 CIDR to Subnet Mask Converter
seo_content:
The /25 CIDR prefix divides a standard Class C network into two equal segments. It uses a subnet mask of 255.255.255.128.

This is often used by network administrators to isolate different departments or guest Wi-Fi networks within the same physical infrastructure. You get 126 usable host addresses per subnet. It is a great way to reduce broadcast traffic and improve network security.

title: /30 CIDR to Subnet Mask Converter
seo_content:
A /30 subnet is very specific: it is designed for point-to-point links. With a mask of 255.255.255.252, it provides only 2 usable IP addresses.

This is the gold standard for connecting two routers directly, as it wastes no IP addresses. Engineers use /30 prefixes for WAN links and serial connections where only the two ends of the connection need an identity.

title: /32 CIDR (Host Mask) Details
seo_content:
A /32 prefix represents a single specific IP address, also known as a host mask. The subnet mask is 255.255.255.255.

In routing tables, a /32 route is the most specific possible path. It is commonly used for Loopback interfaces on routers or to define a specific firewall rule for one single machine. There is no 'network' here, just one unique host.
